notes: Strong test suite using Swift Testing framework (104 files using @Test/@Suite/#expect — the modern Swift 5.9+ framework). 105 test files against 178 GargantuaCore source files gives a 0.59 test-to-source ratio (healthy, > 0.5). Integration and smoke tests exist (RemnantRuleSetIntegrationTests, DefaultProcessRunnerIntegrationTests, MCPCleanToolHandlerIntegrationTests, MCPStdioPhase3IntegrationTests, AppScannerSmokeTests, MLXBackendPerformanceSmokeTests). Zero skip markers. No .only markers. No coverage report available so exact line/branch coverage is unknown.

findings.title: No test coverage report generated — actual line/branch coverage unknown
findings.description: The test suite has 105 files and a healthy 0.59 test-to-source ratio, but no code coverage report exists in the repo. Without a coverage report it is impossible to verify which source lines are actually exercised. Swift's built-in coverage support (`--enable-code-coverage` in `swift test`) can generate LLVM coverage data that can be converted to lcov format for CI reporting.
findings.snippet: # No coverage report found at: coverage/lcov.info, .build/**/*.profdata, etc.
findings.fix_prompt:
# Fix: Enable and collect test coverage data in CI

## Context
The project has a healthy test suite but generates no coverage report. Adding `--enable-code-coverage` to `swift test` produces LLVM profiling data that can be converted to lcov for dashboards and CI enforcement.

## Location
- File: Package.swift (project-wide configuration)
- Dimension: testing / major

## Required change
1. Run tests with coverage:
   ```bash
   swift test --enable-code-coverage
   ```
   This writes `.build/debug/codecov/*.profraw` and `.build/debug/codecov/*.profdata` files.

2. Convert to lcov format:
   ```bash
   xcrun llvm-cov export \
     -format=lcov \
     .build/debug/GargantuaPackageTests.xctest/Contents/MacOS/GargantuaPackageTests \
     -instr-profile .build/debug/codecov/default.profdata \
     -ignore-filename-regex='.build|Tests' \
     > coverage.lcov
   ```

3. Add to CI pipeline (GitHub Actions or the existing `.githooks/pre-commit`):
   ```yaml
   - name: Test with coverage
     run: swift test --enable-code-coverage
   - name: Export lcov
     run: |
       xcrun llvm-cov export \
         -format=lcov \
         .build/debug/GargantuaPackageTests.xctest/Contents/MacOS/GargantuaPackageTests \
         -instr-profile .build/debug/codecov/default.profdata \
         -ignore-filename-regex='.build|Tests' > coverage.lcov
   ```

4. Add `coverage.lcov` to `.gitignore` (it's an artifact, not source).

## Constraints
- This is build/CI configuration, not source code. Do not change any source files.
- The exact `.xctest` bundle name may differ; check `.build/debug/` for the correct name.
- On Apple Silicon Macs, `xcrun llvm-cov` is provided by Xcode Command Line Tools.

## Verification
- Run: `swift test --enable-code-coverage && ls .build/debug/codecov/`
- Run the lcov export command and verify the file is non-empty.
- Run: `/tenet-skills:tenet-toolchain` to pick up the new coverage file.

findings.title: 4 security/privacy services lack unit tests: CodeSignatureVerifier, CloudAIRedaction, PermissionChecker, ClaudeCodeAgentSessionController
findings.description: Four services without dedicated test files have elevated security or privacy relevance: (1) CodeSignatureVerifier — validates executable code signatures before running (security invariant); (2) CloudAIRedaction — strips sensitive data before sending to cloud AI (privacy invariant); (3) PermissionChecker — validates OS entitlements; (4) ClaudeCodeAgentSessionController — manages the AI agent lifecycle including process spawning. All four are covered by higher-level integration tests but lack unit tests that could catch regressions in edge cases.
findings.snippet: // CodeSignatureVerifier.swift, CloudAIRedaction.swift, PermissionChecker.swift, ClaudeCodeAgentSessionController.swift — no *Tests.swift counterpart in Tests/
findings.fix_prompt:
# Fix: Add unit tests for security/privacy-critical services

## Context
Four services with security or privacy relevance lack dedicated unit tests. Higher-level integration tests provide some coverage, but unit tests provide faster feedback and explicit documentation of expected behavior (including edge cases and failure modes).

## Location
- Files: Sources/GargantuaCore/Services/CodeSignatureVerifier.swift, CloudAIRedaction.swift, PermissionChecker.swift, ClaudeCodeAgentSessionController.swift
- Dimension: testing / minor

## Required change
1. **CodeSignatureVerifier** — test with a known-good binary (e.g., `/bin/ls`) and a known-invalid signature (a modified copy):
   ```swift
   @Test func verifySystemBinary() {
       let verifier = CodeSignatureVerifier()
       #expect(verifier.verify(URL(fileURLWithPath: "/bin/ls")) == true)
   }
   ```

2. **CloudAIRedaction** — test that sensitive patterns are redacted, and non-sensitive content is passed through:
   ```swift
   @Test func redactsAPIKeys() {
       let text = "My token is ghp_ABCDEF123456"
       #expect(!CloudAIRedaction.redact(text).contains("ghp_ABCDEF"))
   }
   ```

3. **PermissionChecker** — test the happy path (checking accessible permissions) and verify the result type structure.

4. **ClaudeCodeAgentSessionController** — unit test init/lifecycle state transitions using a mock `ClaudeCodeAgentProcessExecuting`.

## Constraints
- Do not require special OS permissions in the test target (no full disk access, etc.).
- Mock system calls where needed using the existing protocol abstractions.
- Use the Swift Testing framework (`@Test`, `#expect`) consistent with the rest of the suite.

## Verification
- Run: `swift test --filter CodeSignatureVerifierTests`
- Run: `swift test --filter CloudAIRedactionTests`
- Run: `swift test --filter PermissionCheckerTests`
- Run: `swift test --filter ClaudeCodeAgentSessionControllerTests`

findings.title: Views layer has a 0.33 test-to-source ratio (18 tests / 55 view files)
findings.description: The Views/ directory has 55 source files but only 18 test files (0.33 ratio, below the 0.5 healthy threshold). This is expected for SwiftUI — `@ViewBuilder` bodies and declarative layout code are architecturally difficult to unit test. ViewModels appear to be tested separately. Flagged as info so the team can consciously decide how much view testing is appropriate versus relying on manual testing or integration tests for UI flows.
findings.snippet:
Tests/GargantuaCoreTests/Views/ = 18 test files
Sources/GargantuaCore/Views/ = 55 source files
findings.fix_prompt:
# Optional: Increase SwiftUI view test coverage

## Context
SwiftUI views are architecturally hard to unit test. The 0.33 ratio for the Views layer is lower than the healthy 0.5 threshold but acceptable given SwiftUI's design. The highest-value improvements are:

1. Ensure all ViewModels have test coverage (SmartUninstallerViewModel has tests ✓).
2. For complex computed properties in views (e.g., color logic, enabled states), extract them to testable helper functions or ViewModels.
3. Consider ViewInspector (https://github.com/nalexn/ViewInspector) for selective view unit testing.

## Required change
- No change required — this is a documentation of the posture.
- If the team wants higher confidence: audit the most logic-heavy views (SettingsView, DashboardView, ScanBucketView) for any computed logic that can be extracted to a testable pure function.

## Verification
- Run: `swift test --filter Views` to see current view test count and results.
